message.content:
You are a professional proofreader editing a book manuscript titled "Avoid Misgovernance in Corporates" by Abhik Pal, a retired Indian corporate executive. 

TASK: Read and edit in-place ALL files in /Users/sagarpal/projects/baba_book/chapters/00_frontmatter/

EDITORIAL GUIDELINES:
- Professional, authoritative tone (senior executive addressing management students)
- Use **Indian English** conventions (organisation, programme, honour, etc.)
- **Preserve** the author's voice, anecdotes, personal style, and humor
- **Fix:** spelling errors, grammar, awkward phrasing, sentence construction, punctuation, subject-verb agreement, tense consistency
- **Do NOT change:** domain terms (SBU, BU, CXO, DPR, NIT, LoI, NOA, etc.), Indian business terms, currency (Rs, Cr, Lakhs), YAML frontmatter
- **Do NOT** add or remove content — only improve existing text
- **Do NOT** change chapter headers (lines starting with **)

After editing all files, return a structured change log in this EXACT format (one table per file):

### filename.md
| Location | Type | Original | Edited | Reason |
|----------|------|----------|--------|--------|

List ALL changes you make. Be thorough but conservative.

content.text: I'll start by reading all files in the frontmatter directory.

content.content:
 01_introduction.md
 02_preface.md
 03_about_the_author.md
 04_acknowledgement.md
 05_disclaimer.md

1→---
     2→title: "Introduction to the Book"
     3→part: 0
     4→section: 0
     5→original_lines: "345-350"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**INTRODUCTION TO THE BOOK**
     9→
    10→Thank you for showing interest to read this book. With all honesty I say that I am not a born or a known author. I am a retired industry leader with 36 years of experience in various corporates. I wanted to share my views on Corporate Misgovernance, the title of this book, particularly with management students and young professionals who aspire to be the leaders of future so that they can understand various avoidable decisions taken by leaders in the past and enable them to refrain from committing similar mistakes when they assume top positions in the corporate like Senior Managers, Departmental Heads, Business Unit (BU) & Strategic Business Unit (SBU) Heads, Deputy General Managers (DGM) , General Managers (GM), Assistant Vice Presidents (AVP), Vice Presidents (VP) or the Chief Executive Officers (CXOs) , Presidents & Directors.
    11→
    12→The chapters of the book discusses on several practical issues as I have seen, heard and understood. I hope you will enjoy reading the book.
    13→

1→---
     2→title: "Preface"
     3→part: 0
     4→section: 0
     5→original_lines: "351-394"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**PREFACE**
     9→
    10→I am aware that there are thousands of books in the market on Leadership Management focusing on what Leaders should do illustrating different case studies. Though all these case studies may not be always appropriate in the Indian business environment or in the country where your organization is based, nonetheless they present very important issues handled by leaders, some of which may have had deep bearing on the viability, sustenance and profits of the organization. Leaders must learn lessons from these case studies so as to act appropriately in similar situations in future.
    11→
    12→However, this book revolves the other way round. **It focuses on what Leaders should avoid**. It cites numerous examples of wrong decisions taken by leaders, albeit in my opinion, some of which have led to serious consequences for the organization. As I grew up the ladder and eventually assumed a top position, I have come across various issues myself as well as those which I learnt from my friends who worked in different corporates at senior leadership positions. All these issues led to the compilation of this book which serves as a practical guide for young growing professionals aspiring to become leaders of future in their respective fields of expertise.
    13→
    14→Unlike many theories of management (meant for examinations?), I have narrated issues and cited decisions taken by leaders which were probably wrong in my opinion and led to various problems in the organization. In every such issue discussed, I have also tried to find out what other solution could have been more appropriate so that these problems were arrested in the first place itself.
    15→
    16→We are all human beings. Leaders too are. **“To err is human”.** People do make mistakes in life. So do leaders in organizations. Examples cited in the book are not meant to disrespect or demine any particular leader or refer to any particular individual, but rather to opine how they could have acted in a different manner. I am grateful to those same leaders belonging to various corporates from whom I have learnt so much for the growth and success in my own professional career. But, at the same time, I have analyzed how they could have taken decisions differently in many instances to avoid problems which cropped up subsequently. Some may disagree with my opinions or the solutions mentioned, nevertheless, it will provide them some food for thought on how they would like to act under such circumstances if arising in their future.
    17→
    18→The entire book has been broadly grouped into five Sections and each Section into different Parts. Each Part deals with issues of a particular domain of operations or department. Each issue of the particular domain is discussed under different chapters constituting the Part. The role of the department is first explained in the Part and thereafter each chapter against this domain discusses on a particular issue and the problems aroused and the likely solution. Some issues discussed may seem simple (not to all) and some chapters are small, may be a one pager, but I felt that such “simple or one pager” issues are worth discussing because they are important to an organization. Remember what Shakespeare said:  **“All small incidents in life together create a huge impact on our minds”.** Similarly many small decisions in the corporate may create huge impact on the organization over time. And hence the importance of recognizing issues, big or small. Some of you may already be aware of some of the issues cited and they may feel that these issues are a commonplace not worth mentioning. However, I have sometimes observed that leaders still take such inappropriate decisions and therefore these issues deserve mention and discussion for the benefit of all.
    19→
    20→Almost all the chapters of the book are in reference to construction projects in the Indian context since construction projects encompasses different domains of management. All the chapters are purposefully hilariously titled to draw your attention on the core issue. 
    21→
    22→At the end of each Part, I have also listed some good practices which may be adopted by leaders to create a **“feel good factor”,** a proper corporate governance model and a good working environment amongst employees in the organization. It’s up to you to adopt should you feel it as relevant. 
    23→
    24→In Part 26 of this book, I have explored some new concepts which come across my mind. I leave it to your wisdom to adopt them or ignore. Annexures in Part 27 of the book illustrates some concepts for your better understanding. Part 28 provide some personal advices which are interrelated between your personal and corporate life. These issues are often ignored while we are busy at work in office but they affect your life, family, work, work-life balance and your finances and most importantly your health \- mental, physical and spiritual. Hence it’s importance. Part 29 narrates some lessons I learnt in the corporate which helped me to get along with people at different challenging times.
    25→
    26→As a SBU Head, I had the privilege to work with my BU Heads, Departmental Heads of Strategy, Marketing & Business Development, Advertising, Designs & Engineering, Bidding & Costing, Project Management, Site Operations, Production Management, Supply Chain Management, Fleet and Equipment Management, Stores Management, Quality, Safety, Human Resources, Administration, Contracts & Law, Finance & Accounts, Audit, Information Technology, Learning and Development, Insurance, Ethics, ESG, CSR and learnt the varied dynamics of different domains of operations. These experiences gave me a detailed insight to taking right decisions and helped me to flag different issues discussed in the book.  
    27→   
    28→Right from beginning of my career, I had an inclination to analyze different decisions taken by leaders in various corporates under various circumstances. Such observations were of great interest to me. As I grew up with more experience, I felt that many of these decisions taken could have been different and I always wondered on what could have been done right. After I retired, I thought that the only way I can convey my thoughts on right governance to young professionals and management students was to write this book. 
    29→
    30→Small notes before you start reading the book.  
    31→Use of the words like “He/his/him” also means and includes “she/her’s/her”  
    32→Reference to People in the organization means the employees  
    33→Words like Organization, Company & Corporate are interchangeable.  
    34→Words like “you” may also refer to the organization.  
    35→Words like Project and Contract are interchangeable.  
    36→Words like Order, Award and Contract are interchangeable.  
    37→Words like Bid and Tender are interchangeable.  
    38→Words like vendor, supplier, and subcontractor are interchangeable.  
    39→Words like contractor may mean the organization itself  
    40→Words like specifications, tender or bid documents are interchangeable.  
    41→Words like client and customer are interchangeable.
    42→
    43→Last but not the least, you may mail your suggestions and feedback to [abhik.pal.tata@gmail.com](mailto:abhik.pal.tata@gmail.com) so that I too can learn from your experience and incorporate them as deemed fit in the next edition of the book. I wish all of you a great leadership in future. 
    44→
    45→**Happy reading.**
    46→
    47→**Abhik Pal**  
    48→Former Executive Vice President & SBU Head (2017-2020),   
    49→Tata Projects Limited, India  
    50→Former Head of Business Development, Marketing & Tendering (2007 \- 2017\)  
    51→Tata Projects Limited, India
    52→

1→---
     2→title: "About the Author"
     3→part: 0
     4→section: 0
     5→original_lines: "395-406"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ABOUT THE AUTHOR**
     9→
    10→I am a 1984 Civil Engineering graduate from IIEST, formerly known as Bengal Engineering College, Howrah, West Bengal, a premier Engineering institute of India. I am credited with an Advanced Diploma in Management from IMT, Ghaziabad which also a premier institute of management in India.
    11→
    12→In the 36 years of my career, I had a stint of 30 years in three Tata Group companies. In the later years, I was the Head of Business Development, Marketing and Bidding for 10 years in Tata Projects and subsequently assumed the position of **Executive Vice President & SBU Head** of a specific business domain in Tata Projects for 3 years before I hanged my boots.
    13→
    14→In the initial eight years of my professional life, I was a Design/Structural Engineer involved in the designs of different kind of structures like multistoried buildings, cooling towers, high voltage transmission line towers, etc. using software tools like STAAD and SAP. I was also into hardcore software development for designs of Reinforced Concrete Structures (RCC) using Fortran 77 as the programming language. After a eight year stint in the Designs domain, I was transferred to Marketing department and started my career in the field of management. 
    15→
    16→Outside the corporate world, I am an avid lover of music, particularly instrumental music, and I play the harmonica and guitar even today, which kept me going through my bad days and times of stress while in the corporate.
    17→
    18→I was fortunate to meet and talk to Sir Ratan Tata only once in my life in 2018 during an event on “Quality” of the Tata group in Mumbai and I adored his personality, grace, humbleness, wisdom and knowledge with all his magnificent achievements at the back of my mind. This was one of the best moments of my corporate life, a moment which I would cherish for the rest of my life.
    19→

1→---
     2→title: "Acknowledgement"
     3→part: 0
     4→section: 0
     5→original_lines: "407-416"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ACKNOWLEDGEMENT**
     9→
    10→\- To my wife, Soma, having a Masters degree in Computer Science, for always encouraging me to write this new kind of book. She was witness to what I had gone through during my leadership days and had given critical suggestions from time to time. She was confident that this new kind of book will inspire youngsters to learn and act better as future leaders. Her continuous inspiration over the last two years helped me to complete writing this book.
    11→
    12→\- To my son, Sagar, who with Masters, PhD and a post Doctorate degree obtained in a reputed university of France to his credit and with an initial some years of corporate experience, have encouraged me to narrate my experiences as a senior corporate leader. He knows the value of experiences to youngsters like him which can take them to greater heights in their corporate life. He was instrumental being a critic going through the book to point out what was nicely or badly written. It helped me to modify the contents of the book wherever required and this give shape to the final book.
    13→
    14→\- To my daughter-in-law, Samta, a Management Graduate of a reputed international University in France and having significant corporate experience in management, was always inspirational to me for writing this book on issues related to management. She has always been highly inquisitive to learn on how employees can develop themselves to become great leaders and while narrating many such experiences of mine to her complex questions gave me important insights to cover some issues in the book. She too scrutinised the contents of the book which helped me to reach the final stage of the book.
    15→
    16→\- To my well-wishers and friends, who as senior leaders of different organisations provided various inputs on managerial issues which helped me to incorporate them in the book.
    17→

1→---
     2→title: "Disclaimer"
     3→part: 0
     4→section: 0
     5→original_lines: "417-424"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**DISCLAIMER**
     9→
    10→I reiterate that the issues cited in the book do not refer to any particular leader and is not meant to demine or disrespect any leader. These issues as observed in different corporates including those where my friends worked as senior leaders, have been discussed only to understand the complexities and problems that wrong decisions can create and how decisions could have been taken differently, albeit again, in my opinion.
    11→
    12→The issues are important to be discussed because such issues may crop up in any organization. However, the proposed solutions to these issues which have been mentioned to have created problems in the organization may be differently opined by others; nevertheless, it provides an insight to the youngsters for evoking their thought process. That’s the objective of the book. When these youngsters would become our future leaders, their wisdom and experience will be their best judge to decide.
    13→
    14→The book is not meant for senior leaders because they must have gone through most of these issues at one point of time in their career and they have already attained maturity and wisdom to deal with them. It is meant for management students and young professionals who may be enlightened with different issues that leaders of organisations come across so that they can deal with them first hand when they become leaders in the future.
    15→

I was also into hardcore software development for designs of Reinforced Concrete Structures (RCC) using Fortran 77 as the programming language. After an eight-year stint in the Designs domain, I was transferred to the Marketing department and started my career in the field of management.

content.content:
1→---
     2→title: "Preface"
     3→part: 0
     4→section: 0
     5→original_lines: "351-394"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**PREFACE**
     9→
    10→I am aware that there are thousands of books in the market on Leadership Management focusing on what Leaders should do, illustrating different case studies. Though all these case studies may not always be appropriate in the Indian business environment or in the country where your organisation is based, nonetheless they present very important issues handled by leaders, some of which may have had deep bearing on the viability, sustenance and profits of the organisation. Leaders must learn lessons from these case studies so as to act appropriately in similar situations in future.
    11→
    12→However, this book revolves the other way round. **It focuses on what Leaders should avoid**. It cites numerous examples of wrong decisions taken by leaders, albeit in my opinion, some of which have led to serious consequences for the organisation. As I grew up the ladder and eventually assumed a top position, I came across various issues myself as well as those which I learnt from my friends who worked in different corporates at senior leadership positions. All these issues led to the compilation of this book which serves as a practical guide for young growing professionals aspiring to become leaders of the future in their respective fields of expertise.
    13→
    14→Unlike many theories of management (meant for examinations?), I have narrated issues and cited decisions taken by leaders which were probably wrong in my opinion and led to various problems in the organisation. In every such issue discussed, I have also tried to find out what other solution could have been more appropriate so that these problems were arrested in the first place itself.
    15→
    16→We are all human beings. Leaders too are. **”To err is human”.** People do make mistakes in life. So do leaders in organisations. Examples cited in the book are not meant to disrespect or demean any particular leader or refer to any particular individual, but rather to suggest how they could have acted in a different manner. I am grateful to those same leaders belonging to various corporates from whom I have learnt so much for the growth and success in my own professional career. But, at the same time, I have analysed how they could have taken decisions differently in many instances to avoid problems which cropped up subsequently. Some may disagree with my opinions or the solutions mentioned; nevertheless, it will provide them some food for thought on how they would like to act under such circumstances if arising in their future.
    17→
    18→The entire book has been broadly grouped into five Sections and each Section into different Parts. Each Part deals with issues of a particular domain of operations or department. Each issue of the particular domain is discussed under different chapters constituting the Part. The role of the department is first explained in the Part and thereafter each chapter under this domain discusses a particular issue, the problems that arose, and the likely solution. Some issues discussed may seem simple (not to all) and some chapters are small, maybe a one-pager, but I felt that such “simple or one-pager” issues are worth discussing because they are important to an organisation. Remember what Shakespeare said: **”All small incidents in life together create a huge impact on our minds”.** Similarly, many small decisions in the corporate may create a huge impact on the organisation over time. And hence the importance of recognising issues, big or small. Some of you may already be aware of some of the issues cited and may feel that these issues are commonplace, not worth mentioning. However, I have sometimes observed that leaders still take such inappropriate decisions and therefore these issues deserve mention and discussion for the benefit of all.
    19→
    20→Almost all the chapters of the book are in reference to construction projects in the Indian context since construction projects encompass different domains of management. All the chapters are purposefully hilariously titled to draw your attention to the core issue.
    21→
    22→At the end of each Part, I have also listed some good practices which may be adopted by leaders to create a **”feel good factor”,** a proper corporate governance model and a good working environment amongst employees in the organisation. It is up to you to adopt them should you feel them relevant.
    23→
    24→In Part 26 of this book, I have explored some new concepts which came to my mind. I leave it to your wisdom to adopt them or ignore them. Annexures in Part 27 of the book illustrate some concepts for your better understanding. Part 28 provides some personal advice which is interrelated between your personal and corporate life. These issues are often ignored while we are busy at work in the office, but they affect your life, family, work, work-life balance, your finances, and most importantly your health \- mental, physical and spiritual. Hence their importance. Part 29 narrates some lessons I learnt in the corporate which helped me to get along with people at different challenging times.
    25→
    26→As a SBU Head, I had the privilege to work with my BU Heads, Departmental Heads of Strategy, Marketing & Business Development, Advertising, Designs & Engineering, Bidding & Costing, Project Management, Site Operations, Production Management, Supply Chain Management, Fleet and Equipment Management, Stores Management, Quality, Safety, Human Resources, Administration, Contracts & Law, Finance & Accounts, Audit, Information Technology, Learning and Development, Insurance, Ethics, ESG, CSR and learnt the varied dynamics of different domains of operations. These experiences gave me a detailed insight to taking right decisions and helped me to flag different issues discussed in the book.  
    27→   
    28→Right from the beginning of my career, I had an inclination to analyse different decisions taken by leaders in various corporates under various circumstances. Such observations were of great interest to me. As I grew with more experience, I felt that many of these decisions could have been different and I always wondered what could have been done right. After I retired, I thought that the only way I could convey my thoughts on right governance to young professionals and management students was to write this book.
    29→
    30→Small notes before you start reading the book.
    31→Use of the words “He/his/him” also means and includes “she/hers/her”
    32→Reference to People in the organisation means the employees  
    33→Words like Organisation, Company & Corporate are interchangeable.
    34→Words like “you” may also refer to the organisation.  
    35→Words like Project and Contract are interchangeable.  
    36→Words like Order, Award and Contract are interchangeable.  
    37→Words like Bid and Tender are interchangeable.  
    38→Words like vendor, supplier, and subcontractor are interchangeable.  
    39→Words like contractor may mean the organisation itself
    40→Words like specifications, tender or bid documents are interchangeable.  
    41→Words like client and customer are interchangeable.
    42→
    43→Last but not the least, you may mail your suggestions and feedback to [abhik.pal.tata@gmail.com](mailto:abhik.pal.tata@gmail.com) so that I too can learn from your experience and incorporate them as deemed fit in the next edition of the book. I wish all of you great leadership in the future.
    44→
    45→**Happy reading.**
    46→
    47→**Abhik Pal**  
    48→Former Executive Vice President & SBU Head (2017-2020),   
    49→Tata Projects Limited, India  
    50→Former Head of Business Development, Marketing & Tendering (2007 \- 2017\)  
    51→Tata Projects Limited, India
    52→
